intro: The biggest real distinction between Akamai and Selectel is straightforward: Akamai carries high lock-in while Selectel has moderate lock-in. Akamai sits at pricing from Custom (premium pricing), enterprise plan available and carries high lock-in, while Selectel sits at usage-based pricing and carries moderate lock-in. Akamai is a fit when you need global — typical scenarios include global audience with low latency needs. Selectel is aimed at russian companies needing local iaas, object storage, and managed kubernetes, with workloads like russian cloud iaas with dedicated servers and object storage as the common case. The honest trade-off: Akamai is explicitly not for internal projects; Selectel is not intended for international companies or those with sanctions compliance requirements. Lock-in is the factor most teams underestimate: Akamai and Selectel have different exit paths, and that shapes total cost of ownership over a two-to-three year horizon. Before committing, audit where each tool stores state, which APIs you depend on, and how painful a switch would be six months in.
quick_take: Akamai is for global; Selectel is for russian companies needing local iaas, object; decide based on how much lock-in you can accept.
verdict_a: Choose Akamai if your primary workload is global and you accept higher lock-in for the stack.
verdict_b: Choose Selectel if your primary workload is russian companies needing local iaas, object storage, and managed kubernetes.

faq.a: Akamai publishes an entry price of Custom (premium pricing); Selectel's entry price is not in our data, so a definitive comparison is not possible without a quote from Selectel.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Akamai to Selectel?
faq.a: Migrating from Akamai to Selectel is not trivial — Akamai has high lock-in, which typically means data formats, APIs, or stack assumptions that have to be rewritten. Since both tools sit in the same category (CDN / Edge / Acceleration), the conceptual mapping is closer, but you still need to validate feature parity against your specific workload. Plan migration as a project, not a script: inventory integrations, test on non-production workloads, and keep Akamai running in parallel until Selectel is verified.

Our data gives Akamai and Selectel the same developer experience score (3/5), so neither has a clear edge on DX by that measure. The differentiator is usually how each tool fits your workflow — things like CLI quality, docs, local dev loop, and error messages. The safest call is to pilot both on a representative task and see which one gets out of your way.

faq.q: Is Selectel a good alternative to Akamai?
faq.a: Selectel is in the same category as Akamai (CDN / Edge / Acceleration), so it's a reasonable alternative to evaluate. Selectel is aimed at russian companies needing local iaas, object storage, and managed kubernetes, while Akamai targets global — that difference should drive the switch decision. Note that Selectel is explicitly not for international companies or those with sanctions compliance requirements — if that describes your workload, it's not a viable replacement.
